ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Pad Thai with seafood; mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried noodle dish with seafood — mapped to FoodEx2 composite dish category for stir-fried noodles; no exact Pad Thai code exists in FoodEx2 v2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific Pad Thai entry; A0CKP (composite dish, stir-fried noodles) used as the closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Pad Thai as a food item; left null per ontology honesty policy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Pad Thai Noodle with Seafood weighs approximately 450 g, as reported by the vendor listing on GrabFood, and represents a typical single-person restaurant serving.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Pad Thai / Stir-fried Rice Noodles with Seafood; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual components (rice noodles, prawns, squid, eggs, peanuts)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact 'Pad Thai with Seafood' entry found in HPB Foodo. Values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against HPB stir-fried noodle dish entries and USDA FoodData Central component data for rice noodles (FDC ID 168936), cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180), squid (FDC ID 175168), scrambled egg (FDC ID 172185), and peanuts (FDC ID 172430). The per-100g values are consistent with a mixed stir-fried noodle dish at the stated ingredient ratios. Retained input values with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not reported in the primary source and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (GI ~53–61)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pad Thai with Seafood as a composite dish. GI of 53 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (GI range 53–61; mean ~53 for thin rice noodles, boiled). The dish GI is moderated downward from plain noodles by the presence of protein (prawns, squid, eggs), fat (peanuts, oil), and fibre (bean sprouts, chives), consistent with mixed-meal GI attenuation principles documented in Brand-Miller et al. (2003, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (53) × available carbohydrate per serving (450 g × 16.92 g/100g = 76.14 g) / 100 = 40.4 g GL. This is a high GL serving due to the large portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 338.5 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 1,523 mg sodium per standard 450 g plate — exceeding 66% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and tamarind-based sauce when ordering, retain the crushed peanuts for partial potassium offset, and limit portion size to no more than half a standard plate to keep sodium intake within DASH-diet thresholds.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles are a high-GI refined carbohydrate (GI ~53 for the dish, but GL ~40 per 450 g plate); 16.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 0.9 g dietary fibre results in rapid postprandial glucose absorption, and palm sugar in the sauce further elevates glycemic load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 150–200 g, request reduced palm sugar in preparation, and increase the proportion of seafood and egg relative to noodles to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate ratio and blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is relatively low at 1.23 g per 100 g and trans fat is negligible at 0.03 g per 100 g; however, cholesterol is 53.85 mg per 100 g (approximately 242 mg per 450 g plate) from prawns, squid, and eggs, and sodium is high at 338.5 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the dish's low saturated fat and seafood-based protein are positive attributes, but the high sodium content and moderate cholesterol load warrant portion control and a request for reduced sauce; pairing with a potassium-rich side such as steamed vegetables is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Prawns and squid provide lean protein with some omega-3 fatty acids; garlic chives and lime contribute antioxidants and vitamin C (4.6 mg per 100 g); however, chilli flakes are nightshade-derived and fish sauce sodium may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may tolerate this dish well given its anti-inflammatory seafood and antioxidant components, but should request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium and monitor personal sensitivity to nightshade-derived chilli flakes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pad Thai Noodle with Seafood (ผัดไทยทะเล; 海鲜炒河粉) is a classic Thai stir-fried rice noodle dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and casual Thai restaurants. The dish is prepared by stir-frying thin, flat rice noodles in a wok over high heat with a savoury-sweet-tangy sauce made from fish sauce, tamarind paste, and palm sugar. The seafood component typically consists of prawns and squid, cooked alongside beaten eggs that are scrambled into the noodles during stir-frying. Bean sprouts and garlic chives are folded in at the end to retain their crunch, and the dish is finished with crushed roasted peanuts and a lime wedge served on the side. The texture is a combination of soft, slightly chewy noodles, tender seafood, and crisp bean sprouts. The flavour profile is umami-forward with a balance of saltiness from fish sauce, mild sweetness from palm sugar, and brightness from lime and tamarind. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 146 kcal, 6.9 g protein, 5.4 g fat (1.2 g saturated), 16.9 g carbohydrate, 0.9 g dietary fibre, and 338 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approximately 1,523 mg per standard 450 g plate) is a primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The refined rice noodle base carries a medium glycemic index (~53) but a high glycemic load per serving (~40), making portion control essential for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and gluten-free in its traditional form, and contains major allergens including crustaceans, molluscs, eggs, peanuts, and fish.

confidence_reason: No exact 'Pad Thai with Seafood' entry was found in HPB Foodo or any other single approved primary source. Per-100g nutrient values originate from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and were cross-validated for plausibility against HPB stir-fried noodle dish entries and USDA FoodData Central component data for rice noodles, cooked shrimp, squid, scrambled egg, and peanuts. Values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for the described dish at the stated ingredient ratios. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles, moderated for mixed-meal composition. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, mineral micronutrient profiles, and moisture data for this composite stir-fried noodle dish are not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite dish entry as of April 2026. 2. NUTRIENT PROVENANCE: Core macro and secondary nutrient values retained from input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated against HPB and USDA component data; no single approved source provides an exact match. 3. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for thin rice noodles; the composite dish GI is expected to be at or below this value due to protein, fat, and fibre from seafood, eggs, and peanuts. 4. HALAL STATUS: Not confirmed halal as fish sauce brand and preparation practices are vendor-specific; marked false pending vendor certification. 5. PORTION: Two portions were listed in the input (450 g plate and 650 g set meal); the 450 g plate is used as the default serving for GL calculation. The 650 g set meal GL would be approximately 58.4. 6. ETHNIC TAG CORRECTION: Input listed 'Curry' as an ethnic tag, which is incorrect for Pad Thai; this has been removed. 7. ALIASES CONSISTENCY: All strings in aliases_by_language have been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full RESULT JSON from input. Identified missing Thai native-script name, missing multilingual aliases, missing health_context for cardiovascular_disease, missing GI data, and incomplete ontology.

Iteration 2: Added Thai native-script (ผัดไทยทะเล) and romanized (Phat Thai Thale) to local_names. Added Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi local names. Populated full aliases and aliases_by_language with consistency check. Added GI 53 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate with GL calculation. Added cardiovascular_disease health context. Corrected food_name to canonical English. Removed incorrect 'Curry' ethnic tag. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes. Verified all aliases_by_language strings appear in flat aliases array. Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values. Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 53 falls within the low GI range (<=55). Scored 8/8 — PASS.
